sections.title: Edge Middleware — The Front Door
sections.content:
Every API request enters through Next.js Edge Middleware. This is the first code that runs — before any route handler. It extracts the JWT session (if present), runs the rate limiter keyed by user ID or IP, and forwards the request with `x-user-id` and `x-team-id` headers injected for downstream handlers.

Note that public paths (sign-in, sign-up, magic link, Stripe webhooks, health check) are explicitly exempted from authentication in `src/api/middleware/auth.ts`.

sections.title: JWT Authentication — Signing and Verifying Tokens
sections.content:
The JWT module is the core of the auth system. `signToken()` creates a token with the user's ID, email, team ID, and role, signed with the `JWT_SECRET` env var and a 7-day expiry. `verifyToken()` validates incoming tokens on every request.

The `TokenPayload` interface defines the shape of the JWT claims — this is the session context available throughout the request lifecycle.

sections.title: Session Management — Cookies and Token Lifecycle
sections.content:
Sessions are managed via an `acme-session` httpOnly cookie with a 7-day max age (matching the JWT TTL — REQ-AUTH-002). The `getSession()` function extracts and verifies the cookie on each request, while `setSession()` writes it after successful login.

`optionalSession()` provides a non-throwing variant for routes that work with or without authentication.

sections.title: Data Models — Zod Schemas as Source of Truth
sections.content:
All entity types are defined using Zod schemas that serve double duty: they are TypeScript types at compile time and runtime validators for API input. The `User`, `Team`, and `Invitation` models live in `src/db/models/`.

Notice the `PLAN_LIMITS` constant in `team.ts` — it defines the usage and member caps per plan tier, used by both the team service (member limit enforcement) and the billing service (plan activation).

sections.title: Database Queries — Supabase CRUD Layer
sections.content:
Each entity has a dedicated query module in `src/db/queries/`. These functions create a Supabase admin client (using the service role key for unrestricted access) and perform typed queries.

The `createTeam()` function is noteworthy — it inserts the team AND creates the owner's membership in sequence. This is a potential failure point since there's no transaction wrapper, but Supabase's RLS is bypassed via the service role key.

sections.title: Auth Service — Orchestrating Sign-In Flows
sections.content:
The auth service sits between API routes and the data layer, orchestrating the full sign-in lifecycle. `signIn()` looks up the user by email, verifies the password via bcrypt, finds their teams, signs a JWT, and sets the session cookie.

The magic link flow is split across `requestMagicLink()` (generates token + sends email) and `redeemMagicLink()` (consumes token + creates session). If the magic link email doesn't match an existing user, a new account is auto-created.

sections.title: Billing Service — Stripe Integration
sections.content:
Billing is handled through Stripe's Checkout Sessions and Webhooks pattern. `createCheckoutSession()` creates or retrieves a Stripe customer, then opens a checkout session with the selected plan price.

The webhook handler (`handleWebhook`) processes three critical events: `checkout.session.completed` (activate subscription), `customer.subscription.deleted` (downgrade to free), and `invoice.payment_failed` (logging only). The webhook signature is verified using Stripe's SDK to prevent spoofing.

sections.title: Rate Limiter — In-Memory Sliding Window
sections.content:
The rate limiter implements a simple sliding-window algorithm using an in-memory `Map`. Each key (user ID or IP) gets 100 requests per 60-second window (REQ-API-002). Expired entries are cleaned up every 5 minutes.

This design is intentionally simple — it works for single-instance deployments. For multi-instance production, you'd swap this for a Redis-backed implementation. The `cleanupExpiredEntries()` function prevents memory leaks from abandoned keys.

sections.title: Error Handling — Typed Error Hierarchy
sections.content:
All application errors extend `AppError` which carries a machine-readable `code`, human-readable `message`, and HTTP `status`. Specialized subclasses (`AuthenticationError`, `AuthorizationError`, `NotFoundError`, `RateLimitError`, `ValidationError`) set appropriate defaults.

The `errorResponse()` function converts any error into a structured JSON response. Known `AppError` types return their specific status; unknown errors return a generic 500 to avoid leaking internal details.
